machine_controls.label: Validation Strictness
machine_controls.selection_semantics: Reference-only machine control; downstream runtimes may compile their own strictness profiles.
machine_controls.definition: How strongly preservation, consistency, and tractability claims are validated before a transformation is accepted.

machine_controls.label: Validation Strictness
machine_controls.definition: How strongly feasibility, tractability, and solution-quality claims are validated before optimization outputs are accepted.

machine_controls.label: Validation Strictness
machine_controls.definition: How strongly calibration, predictive adequacy, and uncertainty claims are validated.

machine_controls.label: Validation Strictness
machine_controls.definition: How strongly identifiability, forward-consistency, and stabilization claims are validated.

machine_controls.label: Validation Strictness
machine_controls.definition: How strongly rule correctness, conflict handling, and traceability are validated.

machine_controls.label: Provenance Mode
machine_controls.definition: How aggressively provenance is retained and exposed when fusion reconciles or aggregates multiple sources.

machine_controls.label: Validation Strictness
machine_controls.definition: How strongly coherence, conflict, and interface claims are validated in fused outputs.

machine_controls.label: Validation Strictness
machine_controls.definition: How strongly model fidelity, calibration, interface, and execution stability are validated.

machine_controls.label: Validation Strictness
machine_controls.definition: How strongly update quality, diversity, and carryover claims are validated.

design_notes: Machine controls are not paradigm semantics. They govern validation strictness, provenance exposure, and related execution policy choices that should stay separate from the ontology itself.
Validation strictness is published for every paradigm because downstream systems commonly need a shared handle for how much evidence they demand before accepting a stage result.

Fusion additionally exposes Provenance Mode because reconciliation and aggregation across multiple sources often require an explicit policy for how source lineage is retained or surfaced; other paradigms may still record provenance artifacts, but they do not usually need a paradigm-specific provenance-mode control.
